content:
Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving Fanmengjing Wang a , Kaiqiang He a

, Zhouyou Wang a, Hongyu Ma a

, Fengyan Shi a, Zhengchen Li a , Xiangwen Zhou b

, Qi Wu a, Durga Acharya c, Cara M. Doherty c

, Matthew R. Hill b, Zhikao Li a

, Huanting Wang a,* a Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Monash University, Clayton, Victoria, 3800, Australiab Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Monash University, Clayton, Victoria, 3800, Australiac Manufacturing, Research Unit, CSIRO, Clayton, VIC, 3168, Australia

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: A B S T R A C T There is an increasing demand for lithium (Li) recovery driven by the expansion of renewable energy storage and electric vehicles. New technologies that provide a more eco-friendly and cost-effective process for Li extraction are urgently needed to complement the existing methods for lithium extraction in the industries (e.g. hard rock mining and precipitation), and membrane-based separation is an emerging alternative. Polymeric ion-selective membranes, offering advantages such as widespread availability and good processability, are considered to have more potential for large-scale application compared to the microporous materials-based membranes; however, the efficient separation of monoand divalent ions remains challenging for polymeric membranes, primarily due to the difficulties in con­trolling their pore and channel structures. In this work, we prepared a polymeric membrane using poly (acrylic acid) (PAA) and polyamide (PA) featuring geometrically asymmetric ion-transport channels. The ion-transport channels in the PAA-PA membrane are gradually confined as they progress from the PES layer to the PAA layer, and ultimately to the PA layer. Owing to the synergistic effect of the ion-selective functional groups of PAA and the size-sieving effect of PA, the PAAPA membrane achieved a Li+/Mg2+ selectivity of 43.3 (separation factor).

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: -selective functional groups of PAA and the size-sieving effect of PA, the PAAPA membrane achieved a Li+/Mg2+ selectivity of 43.3 (separation factor). This work introduces a new design for asymmetric ion-transport channels, advancing the development of polymeric membranes for high-performance lithium extraction.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: 1. Introduction The high demand for lithium (Li) for making Li-ion batteries is of great significance in the fields of renewable energy storage and electric vehicles, and has led to increased efforts towards research in advanced technologies for lithium recycling and extraction [1–4]. Current indus­trial technologies for lithium recovery include traditional hard rock mining and extractions from lake brines and sea water like adsorption, solvent extraction, and precipitation. The high energy consumption of the aforementioned processes, and their environmental-unfriendliness (e.g. waste management and the employment and regeneration of haz­ardous materials) have triggered the developments of other approaches for lithium recovery [5–11]. An emerging alternative for lithium extraction is membrane-based separation that has the advantages of high energy-efficiency and low cost, and hence the potential for large-scale application [12–14]. A major challenge in lithium extraction from aqueous resources is the limited size difference between lithium ion (Li+: 7.6 Å) and other coexisting ions (e.g. Magnesium ion (Mg2+: 8.6 Å)) in brines, sea water, and other lithium-containing solutions leached out from ores or recycled lithium products.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: ns (e.g. Magnesium ion (Mg2+: 8.6 Å)) in brines, sea water, and other lithium-containing solutions leached out from ores or recycled lithium products. To precisely control the size of the ion-selective channels in the membranes, pore aperture-controllable porous mate­rials like metal organic framework (MOF) and covalent organic frame­work (COF) have become popular for making ion separation membranes, which demonstrated good ion selectivity. However, the complexity and high-cost in making porous materials and the difficulties in incorporating these materials into defect-free membranes have hin­dered their development towards large-scale fabrication and application [15–21]. The traditional polymeric materials, on the other hand, are more suitable for making industrial applicable ion-selective membranes due to their low cost, wide availability, and good processability. Nevertheless, the wide pore-size distribution and non-confined channels of the polymer-based membranes due to the flexibility of polymer chains remain challenging issues yet to be overcome, and advancement in membrane design in both materials and structure is needed for achieving high separation performance of polymer-based, ion-selective membranes [22–26]. The design and modification of ion-transport channels in membranes

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: Available online 8 October 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). into geometrically and/or functionally asymmetry, inspired by the asymmetric biological channels that enable directional ion flow and regulate selective ion transport, has proven to be an effective strategy in enhancing the membrane performance in ion sieving and power gen­erations [27–32]. The heterogeneous structures provide regulated and dominant direction of ion channels that improve the efficiency of ion migration/diffusion in the membrane; furthermore, the confined envi­ronment created in the asymmetric channel is favourable for effective ion sieving, gating, and rectification [32]. Current asymmetric ion-transport channels for membranes are mainly composed of MOF/porous materials hybrids, MOF/polymer hybrids, and graphene oxide (GO)/polymer hybrids [27,28,30,32]. Polymer-based asymmetric ion-transport channels are underexplored and deserve focused research and development due to their low cost and scalability. The negatively charged carboxyl groups (-COO-) is a commonly used ion selective functional groups incorporated in cation exchange mem­branes (CEMs), which demonstrates stronger binding affinity for diva­lent ions (e.g. Mg2+ and calcium ions (Ca2+)) than for the monovalent ions (e.g. Li+ and potassium ion (K+)) [33].

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: stronger binding affinity for diva­lent ions (e.g. Mg2+ and calcium ions (Ca2+)) than for the monovalent ions (e.g. Li+ and potassium ion (K+)) [33]. Current methods for inducing membranes with carboxyl groups mainly relies on functional­izing membranes using carboxyl group-containing monomers or moi­eties, and the complex process requires precise control of the reactions and may result in weakened mechanical strength of the membranes [22,34,35]. The direct use of carboxylic polymers as membrane materials, which is rarely reported, would potentially provide a more effective functionalization and scalable membrane compared to post-functionalization of membranes with carboxyl groups. The commercially-available poly acrylic acid (PAA) possess abundant carboxyl groups and have been reported to be employed in constructing ionic channels, for example, an ion-conductive eutectogel composed of PAA and the hydrophobic poly(hexafluorobutylacrylate) [36]. Howev­er, due to its high hydrophilicity and the lack of confined ion-transport channels, it is difficult to apply pure PAA membranes in aqueous separation. Herein in this work, we designed a poly (acrylic acid)-polyamide (PA) membrane supported by poly ether sulfone (PES) substrate for lithium-ion extraction in an aqueous environment.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: ed a poly (acrylic acid)-polyamide (PA) membrane supported by poly ether sulfone (PES) substrate for lithium-ion extraction in an aqueous environment. PA films can be readily prepared with dense networks via interfacial polymerization of amines in an aqueous solution and chlorides in an organic phase; therefore, they exhibit good structural stability and are suitable for in­dustrial use [37–40]. As illustrated in Fig. 1, the PAA-PA membrane exhibits geometrically asymmetric ion-transport channels and they are gradually confined progressing from the PES layer to the PAA layer, and ultimately to the PA layer. The PAA-PA membrane was prepared in two steps: Firstly, the growth of PAA on a cellulose nanofiber (CNF) coated

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: PES substrate via free radical polymerization, and then the interfacial polymerization of polyamide consisting of m-Phenylenediamine (MPD) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) monomers on the PAA layer. In the voltage-driven diffusion test of the PAA-PA membrane, the PES layer was oriented toward the mixed-ion feed solution (molar ratio Li+: Mg2+ = 1:1). The combined effects of ion-selective functional groups of PAA and the size-sieving properties of the PA layer contributed to a boosted Li+/Mg2+ selectivity of 43.3 for the membrane. 2. Experimental method 2.1. Materials Acrylic acid (AA) monomers, N,N′-Methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), ammonium persulfate (APS), m-Phenylenediamine (MPD, 99 %), tri­mesoyl chloride (TMC, 98 %), n-hexane, N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), lithium chloride (LiCl), and magnesium chloride (MgCl2) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Bayswater, VIC, Australia). 2.2. Preparation of the PAA-PA membrane Cellulose nanofiber (CNF) aqueous solutions were prepared using a method described in the literature [41]. The commercial poly ether sulfone substrate (PES, pore size (dry state): 30 nm, Sterlitech, Auburn, WA, USA) was first treated with the CNF aqueous solution (0.2 wt%) for 2 min, and the excess solvent was removed using a rubber roller after treatment. The treated PES substrate was then immersed in the aqueous mixture of acrylic acid monomer, MBA, and APS for another 2 min before undergoing a heat treatment of 70 ◦C for 24 h.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: was then immersed in the aqueous mixture of acrylic acid monomer, MBA, and APS for another 2 min before undergoing a heat treatment of 70 ◦C for 24 h. After the formation of poly acrylic acid (PAA) on the substrate, the growth of polyamide (PA) coating layer was conducted: The PAA membrane was first immersed in the MPD aqueous solution and then in the hexane solution of TMC for interfacial polymerization to occur. The PAA-PA membrane then underwent a heat treatment at 80 ◦C for 24 h and was stored in water before use. 2.3. Characterisation of the PAA-PA membrane Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Thermo Scientific Verios 5 UC Field Emission Gun (FEG) SEM) was used to study the morphology and microstructure of the PA-PAA membranes. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analyses of the membranes were conducted using another FE SEM: Quanta 3D. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analyses of the membrane were performed using a PerkinElmer Spectrum Two FT-IR spectrometer. The surface roughness of the

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: Fig. 1. Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure of the PAA-PA membrane and the asymmetric ion-transport channel in the membrane for Li+/ Mg2+ separation. F. Wang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 738 (2026) 124807 membranes was studied using the atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon AFM, tapping mode). A contact angle measuring instrument Dataphysics OCA15 was used to analyze the wettability of the membranes. The zeta potential of the PAA-PA membranes was analysed using an electrokinetic analyser for solid surface analysis (Anton Paar SurPASS 3, 0.01 M KCl electrolyte, pH = 5.7). The free-volume of the isolated PA layer was investigated using Positron Annihilation Lifetime Spectroscopy (PALS, Ortec EG&G Spec­trometer). To prepare samples for PALS analysis, the PES substrates were first removed by immersing the PA/PES membranes in DMF, which fully dissolved PES, and thin films of PA were left on the liquid surface [42]. Samples for PALS analyses were then made by collecting and forming the PA films into spheres (radius: 2.5 mm). 3.4 MBq 22NaCl was used as positron source, sealed by Mylar (source correction 1.60 ns and 2.67 %), and was placed in between two PA spheres. A minimum of five spectra were collected each with 1 * 106 counts to ensure there were no changes with time.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: and was placed in between two PA spheres. A minimum of five spectra were collected each with 1 * 106 counts to ensure there were no changes with time. The spectra were fitted using LT-v9 software, the ortho-positronium pick-off annihilation (long-lifetime component, τ3) was extracted from the spectrum and was then converted into the free volume (r, nm) in the sample using the Tao-Eldrup model: τ3 = 1/2 (1 -r/(r + Δr) + 1/2π sin[2π r/(r + Δr)])-1 where Δr is an empirical parameter for electron layer thickness of 0.166 nm [43]. The Molecular-weight Cutoff (MWCO) values of the PAA and PES substrate were determined by testing the rejection (R, %) of the PAA and PES for Polyethylene glycol (PEG) molecules with a series of Mw (200, 300 and 600 kDa, and 1 and 4 MDa): R (%) =(Cf -Cp /Cf )* 100% where C represents the total organic carbon (TOC) of the fed PEG so­lutions and C stands for the TOC of the corresponding permeate. TOC was determined using a TOC analyser (TOC-LCSH, Shimadzu). By plotting the R versus Mw of the PEGs being tested, the MWCO of the membrane was determined by identifying the Mw for which the mem­brane showed 90 % rejection. The estimated pore size of the membrane was then calculated using an empirical equation [44]: Pore radius (nm) = 0.02 * MWCO0.53 2.4. Ion separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane The ion separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane was tested using a pair of homemade diffusion cells made of Teflon (Cell volume: 15 mL).

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: embrane The ion separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane was tested using a pair of homemade diffusion cells made of Teflon (Cell volume: 15 mL). The ion separation process was voltage-driven (1 V), and the power was supplied by a picoammeter (ITECH IT6322 Triple Channel DC Power Supply) with Ag/AgCl electrodes being placed in the feed and permeate ends. 10 mL of LiCl/MgCl2 aqueous solution (0.1 M, molar ratio of Li + to Mg2+ = 1) was used as feed, and 10 mL of milli Q water was fed in the permeate end. The permeate was collected and then diluted using 2 % nitric acid (HNO3) by 50 times before being sent for analyses using an Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) machine. The separation factor (SF, α) of the membrane was calculated using the following equation: α = CLi+ CMg2+ where C Li+ and C Mg2+ stand for the concentration of lithium and magnesium ions in the permeate solution, respectively. The ion flux (J) was figured out using the following equation: J = CM * VA * t where V stands for the volume of the permeate end (L), A is the active area of the membrane (m2), and t is the separation duration (h). 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Preparation and characterization of the PAA-PA membrane The preparation of the PAA-PA membrane followed two steps: First is the growth of poly (acrylic acid) on the CNF-coated PES substrate, and the next step is the interfacial polymerization of MPD and TMC mono­mers supported by the previously formed PAA-PES membrane.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: oated PES substrate, and the next step is the interfacial polymerization of MPD and TMC mono­mers supported by the previously formed PAA-PES membrane. Coating CNF layers on the PES substrate promoted the presentation of acrylic acid monomers on the substrate surface, and ensured the growth of a continuous poly (acrylic acid) layer on the PES surface as shown by the SEM images of the surface of PAA layers grown with and without CNF coatings (Fig. 2a–b and Supporting Information Figure S1). Besides, the zeta-potential of the PAA layers prepared with and without CNF coating was also measured, and the PAA–CNF–PES membrane showed a more negative zeta potential with -28.93 mV, whereas the PAA-PES mem­brane showed a zeta potential of -19.13 mV (Fig. 4b and Supporting Information Table S1). This result further confirmed that with the addition of CNF layer, the surface availability of PAA was enhanced, which contributed to an increased density of charged functional groups (COO-) in the membrane. By taking SEM images of the pristine PES substrate and the PAA-PA membrane (Fig. 2a and c), it is confirmed that a continuous layer was formed on the PES platform, showing a typical morphology of poly­amides with leaf-like features.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: a and c), it is confirmed that a continuous layer was formed on the PES platform, showing a typical morphology of poly­amides with leaf-like features. A more obvious presence of the leaf-like feature was seen for PA grown on pristine PES substrate without poly (acrylic acid) layer (Supporting Information Figure S2), which is consistent with previous findings that comparatively larger pores of substrate facilitates the formation of the ‘leaf’ structures [39]. By con­ducting AFM analyses on both the surface of the PAA-PA membrane and the PA-PES membrane, the difference in surface roughness of the two membranes is also seen: roughness of the PA-PES membrane (Rq = 75 nm) is greater than the PAA-PA membrane (Rq = 65 nm), which is attributed to the existence of densely presented leaf-like features (Supporting Information Figure S3 and Fig. 2d). A difference in hydro­philicity of the two ‘skin’ layers of the PAA-PA membrane, PA and PES, has been observed. Through measuring the water contact angle of the two sides of the membrane, it is shown that the PA side is hydrophobic (water contact angle (CA): 105◦) while the PES end is hydrophilic with a CA of 70.4◦(Fig. 4a). SEM images of the cross-sectional areas of the PAA-PA membranes, as illustrated in Fig. 3a and b, presented the PA and PAA functional layers with an averaged thickness of approximately 1.7 μm and also the PES substrate supporting the active layers.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: presented the PA and PAA functional layers with an averaged thickness of approximately 1.7 μm and also the PES substrate supporting the active layers. The PA layer is around 500 nm in thickness (Supporting Information Figure S4) and is thinner than the PAA layer with a thickness around 1 μm. This is as expected as the PA layer was formed via interfacial polymerization that benefits thin film growth in a confined space, while the PAA layer was grown via dip coating and free-radical polymerization. In addition, we conducted EDX line scan and mappings for the cross-sections of the PAA-PA membranes to demonstrate PAA and PA layers from an elemental perspective (Supporting Information Figure S5). Nitrogen (N) and Sulphur (S) are the signature elements for PA and PES layers, respectively, while the PAA layer contains no unique elements different to the other two layers but only C, O, and hydrogen (H, non-detectable by EDX) (Supporting Information Figure S5c and d). In the line scan of the cross-sections of the PAA-PA membrane (Supporting Information Figure S5b), a S, N depleted zone was found, whose position aligns with the area of PAA layer (Fig. 3a and b). We conducted PALS analysis for the isolated PA layer in both dry and wet states in order to understand the free volume within the size se­lective layer of the membrane. In this technique, the emitted positron from the 22Na source interacts with electrons within the sample and

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: F. Wang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 738 (2026) 124807 forms a semi-stable state called ortho-positronium (oPs) and these tend to localise in regions of low electron density (i.e. free volume). The lifetime of the oPs relates to the average size of these cavities using the Tao-Eldrup relationship. The spectra were analysed to determine mean pore size of the free volume cavities in the sample. The pore radius of PA samples was measured in both dry and wet states, and the results were 0.28 ± 0.01 nm and 0.29 ± 0.01 nm, respectively. Since the channels in the PAA and PES layers are nano-to microscale, MWCO measurements were conducted by filtering a series of Poly­ethylene glycol (PEG) molecules with molecular weight (Mw) of 200, 300 and 600 kDa, and 1 and 4 MDa. The feed and permeate solutions were analysed using the TOC analyser. The rejection to PEG molecules was calculated and the MWCO of each sample was defined as the Mw of PEG for which the sample show 90 % rejection (Supporting Information Figure S6). The pore size of the sample was then calculated using an empirical equation r (radius) = 0.02 * MWCO0.53, which gives an estimated value of the free volumes in the membranes. The pore radius for PAA and PES layers under wet conditions were determined to be 28 and 61 nm, respectively. The channel sizes of each component in the PAA-PA membrane are summarized and presented in Fig. 3c.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: were determined to be 28 and 61 nm, respectively. The channel sizes of each component in the PAA-PA membrane are summarized and presented in Fig. 3c. Moreover, the results on the pore sizes of each layer are consistent with the pre­viously done measurements of Li+ flux of the three layers. To examine the permeance of lithium ions through each layer, and the voltagedriven diffusion tests were performed separately for the three layers. As shown in Fig. 3d, the flux of Li+ for the supporting layer PES is 148 mmol m-2 h-1, while for the functional layers PAA and PA, it was 131 and 22.4 mmol m-2 h-1, respectively. The gradual decrease in the flux of lithium ions from PES, to the PAA, and then to the PA layer indicates the progressively confined, asymmetric ion-transport channels in the PAAPA membrane. Elemental analysis and molecular characterization have also been performed for the PAA-PA membrane. EDX analyses were conducted for both the PAA-PA membrane and pristine PES substrate (Fig. 4c and Supporting Information Figure S7 and S8). The main elements composing the PAA-PA membrane are shown to be C (67.4 %), O (11.5 %), N (11.1 %), and S (10.1 %), which are the main elements composing PA, PAA, and PES. The PES substrate was shown to be mainly composed of C (69 %), O (11.6 %), and S (19.4 %), and the presence of nitrogen and a major decrease in the content of sulphur in the PAA-PA membrane further confirmed the formation of the selective layer on the substrate.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: itrogen and a major decrease in the content of sulphur in the PAA-PA membrane further confirmed the formation of the selective layer on the substrate. Molecular characterization of the PAA-PA membrane was demonstrated using the FTIR technique, and the spectrum is shown in Fig. 3d. Sharp and strong absorption bands at around 1600, 1500, 1250, and 720 cm-1 are seen in the spectrum, suggesting the presence of C–N, N–H bending, C–N stretching, and –CH bending vibrations of polyamides. Addition­ally, the minor absorption peaks at around 1700, 1550, and 1465 cm-1 can be attributed to the C––O stretch, COO-, and -CH bending of PAA, confirming its existence in the membrane (Fig. 4d and Supporting In­formation Figure S9). The difference in peak intensities of characteristic bands assigned to PA and PAA is mainly due to the higher content of polyamide in the membrane. 3.2. Asymmetric design and the Li+/Mg2+ separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane 3.2.1. Asymmetric Li+/Mg2+ separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane The mono/di-valent ion separation capability of the PAA-PA mem­brane was demonstrated through voltage-driven diffusion tests using a pair of custom-made Teflon cells. In the diffusion tests, the feed solution consisted of a binary aqueous mixture of LiCl and MgCl2 (0.1 M, 1:1 M ratio), while the permeate end was pure Milli-Q water. The permeate solutions were collected and analysed by ICP to determine the concen­trations of Li+ and Mg2+ in the samples.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: end was pure Milli-Q water. The permeate solutions were collected and analysed by ICP to determine the concen­trations of Li+ and Mg2+ in the samples. The PAA-PA membrane features two skin layers: PES and PA, and diffusion tests were conducted with each of these layers oriented toward the feed solution separately (Fig. 5a and b). As shown in Fig. 5c, the asymmetric ion-transport channels of the

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: PAA-PA membrane has led to an asymmetry in the separation perfor­mance of the PAA-PA membrane. The separation factor of the PAA-PA membrane was four times higher when the PES side was oriented to­wards the feed solution (PES-PAA-PA, SF = 43.3) compared to when the PA side facing the feed (PA-PAA-PES, SF = 11). It is also observed from Fig. 5c that the increase in separation factor when using PES as the ‘entrance’ layer led to a sacrifice in the flux of lithium ions of the PAA-PA membrane, which decreased from 86 to 5.2 mmol m-2 h-1. In addition, PAA-PA membranes prepared without CNF layers were also tested for Li+/Mg2+ separation, and the results showed a significant decrease in separation factors when making membranes without CNF layers to 1.6, demonstrating the significance of CNF coatings for promoting the functionality of PAA layer. To present the potential of the PA-PAA membranes in real-world lithium extractions from brines, where the content of Mg2+ is often found 2 to 50 times higher than Li+, experiments were conducted in separating Li+/Mg2+ with high Mg2+ concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5d, the PA-PAA membranes remained effective Li+/Mg2+ separation with separation factors above 10 under Mg2+/Li+ molar ratios of 10, 20, and 40 in the feed. The flux of Li + decreased with increasing Mg2+/Li+ ratios in the feed compartment, which is as expected due to decrease in the overall content of Li+ and that a portion of ion-transport channels were occupied by the extra Mg2+.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: ment, which is as expected due to decrease in the overall content of Li+ and that a portion of ion-transport channels were occupied by the extra Mg2+. Regarding the long-term separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane, it is seen that the separation factor slightly decreased and then stabilized to 30 while the flux of lithium-ion climbed to 21 mmol m-2 h-1 (Supporting Information Figure S10) after 72 h of operation. From the FTIR spectra of the PAA-PA samples before and after 72 h of operations, it is seen that a broad adsorption band at around 3500 cm-1 occurred after 72 h operation, and this band is assigned to -OH stretching (Supporting Information Figure S11). This result indicates that during the long-term operation of the PAA-PA membrane in an aqueous environment, increased water retention occurred in membrane leading to the formation of hydrogen bonds that potentially occupied the ion-selective sites (COO-). Besides, the zeta potential of the PAA-PA membranes before and after 72 h of operation were measured (Supporting Information Table S1). A significant decrease in the surface charge is seen for the membrane with the zeta potential dropped from -23.76 to -8.75 mV, indicating charge screening of the PAA-PA membrane with the ion-selective sites being gradually occupied and consumed over long-term operation.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: 75 mV, indicating charge screening of the PAA-PA membrane with the ion-selective sites being gradually occupied and consumed over long-term operation. In addition, the SEM images of the PAA-PA membrane surface before and after 72 h showed a membrane surface degradation after a long-term use in the salinity environment with defects being observed on the membrane surface (Supporting In­formation Figure S12). Therefore, the declining selectivity and increasing flux of ions of the PAA-PA membrane during long-time operation is mainly attributed to structural degradation and loosening, ion-exchange with the ion-selective sites, and charge screening. 3.2.2. Li+/Mg2+ separation mechanism of the PAA-PA membrane To investigate the ion separation mechanism of the PAA-PA mem­brane, we also evaluated the ion-separation performance of the two functional layers (PAA and PA) supported by PES substrates, as well as the PES substrate itself. The diffusion test results (Supporting Informa­tion Figure S13) indicate that the PES substrate shows no selectivity towards metal ions, while the PAA and PA layers displayed Li+/Mg2+

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: Fig. 4. Characterizations on the surface properties and elemental analyses of the PAA-PA membranes: (a) water contact angle measurements for the PA and PES sides, (b) zeta-potential analyses for PAA and PAA-PA membranes with different PA thicknesses; (c) EDX spectrum and (d) the FTIR spectrum for elemental and chemical composition studies.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: Fig. 5. Schematic illustrations of testing the PAA-PA membrane in (a) ‘PES-PAA-PA’ direction and in (b) ‘PA-PAA-PES’ direction. Li+/Mg2+ separation performances (separation factor and flux of lithium-ion) of the PAA-PA membranes (c) in different configurations (Mg2+/Li+ = 1:1) and (d) under different Mg2+/Li+ ratios. F. Wang et al. Journal of Membrane Science 738 (2026) 124807 separation factors of 7 and 8, respectively. The ion-selectivity of the PAA layer is mainly attributed to selective interactions of its carboxyl groups with lithium and magnesium ions. Carboxyl groups are found to have stronger binding affinities to divalent ions (Mg2+) than to monovalent ions (Li+) due to higher charge density and more stable hydration shells of Mg2+ ions. When migrating through the PAA layer, the energy barrier to be overcome for Mg2+ was greater than for Li + ions. Therefore, the diffusion of Mg2+ ions was hindered, while the weakly-interacted Li+ ions exhibit greater mobility and, oc­casionally, jump between the sites of deprotonated COO-under external electric fields [45]. The difference in migration/diffusion rates between Li+ and Mg2+ ions contributed to the refinement of lithium ions with a separation factor of 7 upon arrival at the PA functional layer. Different from the PAA layer, the functional PA layer contains no ionselective moieties within its structure.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: upon arrival at the PA functional layer. Different from the PAA layer, the functional PA layer contains no ionselective moieties within its structure. Alternatively, polyamide ach­ieves Li+/Mg2+ separation mainly through size sieving: steric hindrance or size exclusion effect of PA network towards divalent ions is stronger than towards alkali ions when the ions migrating through the dense network, which contributes to a higher content of monovalent ions in the receiving side [46,47]. Furthermore, due to the high charge density of Mg2+, a moderate level of electrostatic interaction of it with the PA layer is possible, and it also led to a lowered conductivity of Mg2+ in the membrane. Therefore, the mono/di-valent ion-separation capability of the PAAPA membrane is attributed to a synergistic effect of ion-selective in­teractions of carboxyl groups with Li+ and Mg2+ ions (PAA layer) and the size-sieving effect of the PA layer: Li+ ions experienced an initial refinement by PAA layer, which resulted in a Li + to Mg2+ molar ratio of 7, and the concentrated binary mixture was further refined in the PA layer, leading to a total separation factor of 43.3. The asymmetry in the Li+/Mg2+ separation of the PAA-PA mem­brane is mainly due to its asymmetric ion-transport channel.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: tal separation factor of 43.3. The asymmetry in the Li+/Mg2+ separation of the PAA-PA mem­brane is mainly due to its asymmetric ion-transport channel. In both scenario of feeding the binary salt solution from the PES or PA side, lithium ions in the solution were concentrated after passing through the first functional layer (SF = 7 for PAA or 8 for PA), while the difference occurred in the next stage: the PA layer created a confined ion-selective environment than the PAA layer did, which led to a more efficient Li+/ Mg2+ separation and a boosted Li+/Mg2+ separation factor to 43.3. The deprotonated COO-in the PAA might have led to swelling of the network in the aqueous environment to some extent [45]. The comparatively loose structure of PAA that created broader ion-transport channels has limited the boosting of Li+/Mg2+ separation, resulting in an SF of 11. To further validate the role of functional layers sequence (PES-PAAPA), an alternative membrane configuration (PES-PA-PAA) was pre­pared by first growing PA layer directly on the PES support, followed by the growth of PAA layer. The PES-PA-PAA membranes were tested for Li+/Mg2+ separation under the same conditions as the PES-PAA-PA membranes. Unlike the PES-PAA-PA membranes showing effective lithium refinement, the PAA-PA-PES membranes displayed limited Li+/ Mg2+ separation capability, with an SF of 6.4 of Li + over Mg2+ and a flux of lithium ions of 1.5 mmol m-2 h-1 (Fig. 5c).

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: ranes displayed limited Li+/ Mg2+ separation capability, with an SF of 6.4 of Li + over Mg2+ and a flux of lithium ions of 1.5 mmol m-2 h-1 (Fig. 5c). The reduced per­formance could be attributed to the difference in ion-transport channel architecture. While the PES-PAA-PA membranes possess a progressively confined channel structure, the PES-PA-PAA membranes resemble an hourglass configuration: the channel narrows from the PES support to the PA layer, then widens again when reaching the PAA layer. As pre­viously discussed, although the PAA layer contains abundant ionselective moieties (COO-), its comparatively loose structure limits its ability to achieve efficient Li+ refinement without additional confine­ments from the PA network. Consequently, positioning the PAA layer at the final stage in the PAA-PA membrane configuration compromised its Li+/Mg2+ separation efficiency. These findings confirm that the syner­gistic effect of PAA and PA layers in separating Li+ from Mg2+ is dependent on their sequential arrangement, with the PES-PAA-PA structure being essential for achieving efficient lithium refinement.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: In addition to various asymmetrically-structured PAA-PA mem­branes, we also prepared PAA-PA membranes with symmetric structure using the contra-diffusion method. Firstly, PAA/PES membrane was prepared using the same method as for the PAA-PA membrane with asymmetric channel. The PAA/PES membrane was then placed verti­cally in the middle of two cells that contained MPD aq. solution and TMC/hexane solution, respectively. The MPD monomers entered the PAA/PES from the PAA end while the TMC monomer entered from the PES side. By doing so, the oil-water interface was controlled to be within the PAA/PES, unlike for the growth of asymmetric channel, the oil-water interface was on the surface of PAA layer. Interestingly, in the FTIR spectrum of the PAA-PA membrane with symmetric channel, the absence of broad adsorption bands at around 1675, 1615 and 1550 cm-1 is seen, which correspond to C––O, N–H bending, and C–N stretching of PAA (Supporting Information Figure S14). Besides, stronger intensity of adsorption bands corresponding to C–N, N–H bending, C–N stretching, and –CH bending vibrations of PA is also seen in the spectrum for the PAA-PA membrane with symmetric channel, indicating a higher content of PA in the resulting membrane compared to the one with asymmetric channel. This is mainly due to the difference in the approaching for growing PA layers.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: in the resulting membrane compared to the one with asymmetric channel. This is mainly due to the difference in the approaching for growing PA layers. In the preparation of asymmetric channel, the MPD monomers were partially loaded in the pores of PAA/PES by immersing the PAA/PES membrane in a MPD aqueous solution, and part of the MPD monomers were left in the solution or being removed by the rubber roller prior to TMC treatment. However, when preparing PAA-PA membranes with symmetric structure, even though MPD and TMC so­lutions with the same concentrations were used, the direct contact of the two phases at the interface created a more intense and reactive envi­ronment for PA formation. We then tested the ion separation perfor­mance of the PAA-PA membrane with symmetric structure, and it showed a decreased separation factor in Li+/Mg2+ separation of 1 and an increased flux of Li+ of around 110 mmol m-2 h-1. One of the reasons is the reduction in the concentration of PAA in the resulting PAA-PA membrane with symmetric channels, which led to decreased density of ion-selective functional groups (COO-). Besides, due to the difference in hydrophilicity of PAA and PA, microscale phase separation of these two polymer networks might have occurred. These have led to a compromised functionality of the two components for Li+/Mg2+ sepa­ration.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: ation of these two polymer networks might have occurred. These have led to a compromised functionality of the two components for Li+/Mg2+ sepa­ration. The poor ion separation performance of the PAA-PA membranes with symmetric structure further validates the significance of building asymmetric channel in the membrane for boosting the synergistic effect of PAA and PA and effective Li+/Mg2+ separation. 3.2.3. Effects of the crosslinking degree of PAA on the ion-separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane PAA-PA membranes composed of different ratios of AA monomer to MBA crosslinkers were prepared to study the effect of crosslinking de­gree of PAA to the Li+/Mg2+ separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane, where the molar ratios tested were 1:9, 1:3, 3:1, and 9:1. From Fig. 6a it is seen that a AA to MBA ratio of 1:3 contributed to the highest separation factor of the PAA-PA membrane of 43.3 and a ratio of 9:1 led to the lowest of 0.35. A molar ratio of 1:9 of AA to MBA led to a PA network with high crosslinking degree but with an insufficient number of functional carboxyl groups in the network. However, when increasing the AA content to 3:1 and 9:1, the separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane was also impacted due to insufficient cross­linking of the hydrophilic network, which may have led to swelling of the network in the aqueous testing environment.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: pacted due to insufficient cross­linking of the hydrophilic network, which may have led to swelling of the network in the aqueous testing environment. Besides, it was found that with a high content of MBA crosslinker the flux of lithium ions was 10 times higher than the other groups. A possible reason is that the electrostatic interaction is weak of ions with the PAA networks that are composed of a limited number of charged groups, and the hindrance to ion migration/diffusion is minimized, leading to a high flux but a low selectivity as well. Therefore, a molar ratio of 1:3 for AA to MBA was found to be optimum that balanced the dense distribution of functional component (AA) and the rigidness of the network.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: 3.2.4. Effects of the thickness of PA on the ion-separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane To study how different thicknesses of the PA layer influence the Li+/ Mg2+ separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane, membranes with varying contents of MPD monomers were prepared, which deter­mined the thickness of the resulting PA. From the SEM images of the cross-sectional areas of the PAA-PA membranes (Supporting Informa­tion Figure S15), it is seen that the thickness of the PA layer increased from around 300 nm to approaching 800 nm when adding the MPD content from 10 to 50 wt%. The Li+/Mg2+ separation capability of the PAA-PA membranes with varying PA thickness was studied. As shown in Fig. 6b, a MPD aqueous solution of 30 wt% was found optimum for achieving the highest separation factor of the PAA-PA membrane of 43.3, for which the PA thickness was measured to be approximately 550 nm. When increasing the MPD content for PA preparation from 10 to 30 wt%, the separation factor increased from 1 to 43.3; however, when kept increasing the content of MPD to 50 wt%, the separation factor dropped to 3.6 (Fig. 6b). To understand the influence of the PA thickness on the membrane property, zeta potential analyses were conducted for the PAA-PA membranes with varying MPD contents (Fig. 4b).

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: nce of the PA thickness on the membrane property, zeta potential analyses were conducted for the PAA-PA membranes with varying MPD contents (Fig. 4b). It is seen that with higher MPD loading (thicker PA layers), the zeta potential of the PAA-PA membranes became less negative, indicating a lowered overall density of charged functional groups in the active layer. This is consistent with the finding that when increasing the MPD contents from 30 to 50 wt%, which led to an increase in layer thickness from 550 to 780 nm, the separation factor of the PAA-PA membranes dropped by approximately 75 % (Fig. 6b). Besides, the barrier toward ion diffusion was impacted as well without sufficient ion-selective moieties, leading to a higher ion flux from 5.2 to 12.5 mmol m-2 h-1. When the MPD content is below 20 wt%, where the thickness is around 300 nm, confinement of ion trans­port channels from PA was weakened and is insufficient to balance the fast lithium-ion transport within the PAA network, leading to an increased Li+ flux by 4 times while a decreased Li+/Mg2+ separation efficiency. Therefore, a MPD content of 30 wt% (layer thickness of 550 nm) has been found to be an optimum condition that balanced the density of ion-selective compartments and effective confinement from PA in the PAA-PA membrane. 3.3.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: ound to be an optimum condition that balanced the density of ion-selective compartments and effective confinement from PA in the PAA-PA membrane. 3.3. Comparison of the ion-separation performance of the PAA-PA membrane with the state-of-the-art ion-separation membranes High fluxes of lithium ions are often observed in polymeric mem­branes (e.g. PES, polyimide, and conjugated microporous polymer) and 2 dimensional (2D) membranes (e.g. graphene oxide) for lithium extraction, ranging from a few hundred to over a thousand mmol m-2 h-1. However, a majority of them demonstrated low separation factors of below 20 (Supporting Information Table S2) [44,48–59]. Due to the highly flexible chain networks, polymer-based ion-selective membranes suffered from issues like wide pore-size distributions and insufficiently confined pore structures, which led to their high flux of ions but low selectivity. 2D nanosheet-based membranes for lithium refinement have more regulated and confined channels; nevertheless, the difficulties in accurate and efficient functionalization have hindered their advance­ment toward effective lithium sieving. The strategy demonstrated in this work that constructing a polymer membrane with an asymmetric ion-transport channel contributed to an enhanced separation factor of the PAA-PA membrane (43.3) by 2–10 times compared to other state-of-the-art ion-selective polymeric and 2D membranes (Supporting Information Figure S16).

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: PAA-PA membrane (43.3) by 2–10 times compared to other state-of-the-art ion-selective polymeric and 2D membranes (Supporting Information Figure S16). Besides, the preparation of the PAA-PA mem­brane is facile, eco-friendly and cost-effective, which makes it more suitable for industrial application compared to the advanced MOF, and COF-based ion-selective membranes [16,18,19,21]. 4. Conclusion The preparation of the PAA-PA membrane exhibiting asymmetric ion-transport channels and its effective performance in monoand divalent ion separation has been demonstrated. The PAA-PA membrane was prepared through a facile two-step process: first, the growth of the PAA layer on a PES substrate and then the interfacial polymerization of the PA layer on the coated membrane. Owing to the asymmetric struc­ture of the membrane with gradual confinements in the ion-transport channel, the PAA-PA membrane displayed a maximum Li+/Mg2+ sepa­ration factor of 43.3 in the voltage-driven diffusion tests using binary feed. This performance surpasses most of the state-of-the-art polymeric and 2D materials-based ion-selective membranes. In addition, effects affecting the membrane performance, including the crosslinking degree of the PAA network and the content of PAA and PA, were also studied in this work. It was found that a molar ratio of AA monomer to MBA crosslinker of 1:3 and a mass ratio of PAA to PA of 0.3 contributed to an optimized membrane performance.

citation.title: Asymmetric design of ion-transport channels in a polymeric membrane for lithium-ion sieving

content: ound that a molar ratio of AA monomer to MBA crosslinker of 1:3 and a mass ratio of PAA to PA of 0.3 contributed to an optimized membrane performance. This work presents a new strategy for designing asymmetric ion-transport channel and contributes to the advancement of polymeric membranes for monoand divalent ion separation. CRediT authorship contribution statement Fanmengjing Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing
